behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: the failure boundary is real watcher coverage of nested paths (OS watcher semantics + watcher library recursion), which is not reliably exposed by unit/module fakes. Rationale B (side-check): demoting to Integration risks Integration->System top-loss because synthetic event injection can pass even when real recursive watch registration is broken. Rejection gate: rejected Integration because behavior text requires true detection of files "inside a subdirectory of the watched root," which can fail only at real filesystem-event wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temporary root directory, create a real nested subdirectory, write a file into it, and assert exactly one transformed output appears in the output directory within timeout.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: this behavior is a local state/time-window decision for one dedup component; no real OS/network/subprocess is required to trigger the failure. Rationale B (side-check): keeping it at Unit avoids unnecessary Integration inflation because no cross-module handoff is required by the behavior text itself. Rejection gate: rejected Integration because the wording is specifically "deduplication logic" for repeated same path within a window, which is testable at a single object/function boundary with a fake clock.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the dedup component with a controlled clock, submit the same path twice within the configured window, and assert first request is accepted and second is discarded; then advance clock beyond window and assert acceptance again.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: the failure is explicitly tied to real OS multi-event emission behavior; mocks can miss event burst/coalescing patterns. Rationale B (side-check): demoting to Integration worsens Integration->System boundary loss because injected duplicate callbacks may not reproduce timing/order characteristics of real emitters. Rejection gate: rejected Integration due to explicit behavior text "operating system emits multiple filesystem events."
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon with real directory watching, perform a write pattern that triggers duplicate events on the host OS (e.g., temp-write+rename or chunked write), and assert exactly one pipeline execution/output for that file path.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: directory deletion/recreation is an OS-level watch invalidation/rebind scenario; only real filesystem behavior exposes this class of failure. Rationale B (side-check): Integration fakes can hide watcher teardown/re-registration bugs, so keeping System protects against Integration->System mislabel. Rejection gate: rejected Integration because the behavior explicitly requires handling live remove/recreate of the watched directory while running.
behaviors.test_description: Launch daemon on a real temp watched directory, delete that directory, recreate it at the same path, then create a new file and verify it is processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: this is primarily cross-module handoff/order logic (event intake -> metadata read -> queue sort -> processor), so Unit is too low for realistic ordering bugs. Rationale B (side-check): no required subprocess/real network/LLM dependency in the behavior text; System is not mandatory if test controls arrival and timestamp metadata in-process across wired modules. Rejection gate: rejected System because behavior text does not require OS-specific timestamp semantics, only that processing order follows creation timestamps.
behaviors.test_description: Wire watcher-event adapter, metadata provider, scheduler, and processor in-process; submit near-simultaneous file events with controlled creation timestamps and assert processing occurs strictly in timestamp order.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: If implementation derives timestamps only from live OS birthtime with no injectable metadata seam, promote this test to System.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: partial-write handling depends on real filesystem timing/event behavior (size stabilization, locks, rename-on-close patterns), which mocks can systematically hide. Rationale B (side-check): Integration demotion risks missing race conditions between writer and watcher; this is a known Integration->System top-loss boundary. Rejection gate: rejected Integration because behavior text explicitly requires correctness when arrival fires during an active write.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on a real watched directory, start writing a large file in chunks with delays, confirm no processing/output before final chunk/close, then confirm processing occurs after write completion.
